main_summary.summary: Jennifer Lawson, born in Fairfield, Alabama in 1946, recounts her formation in a segregated working-class black community shaped by her father's mechanical-repair business and her mother's curtailed teaching career. She describes her education at Fairfield Industrial High School under principal Imanu J. Oliver, her participation in Birmingham's 1963 Kelly Ingram Park demonstrations against the principal's orders, and summer 1963 and 1964 internships at the Hayden Planetarium and Sloan-Kettering cancer research lab in New York. Lawson entered Tuskegee Institute in 1964 on a Gorgas Scholarship and quickly engaged with civil rights work, meeting Charles Gomillion (of Gomillion v. Lightfoot), Almena Lomax, Gwen M. Patton, and classmate Samuel Younge Jr. She volunteered for the November 1964 Mississippi voter registration drive in Jackson, then moved toward SNCC during the Selma-to-Montgomery period, describing her arrest at the Alabama state capitol facing both state troopers and an oncoming Klan group, several days in the Montgomery County jail, and a later Atlanta arrest alongside Karen Edmonds Spellman, Fay Bellamy Powell, and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson. She left Tuskegee in 1966 to work full-time with SNCC in Wilcox and Lowndes Counties, eventually living in the Lowndes Freedom House where late-night conversations with Stokely Carmichael, Ralph Featherstone, Courtland Cox, and Charlie Cobb engaged Frantz Fanon and economic-empowerment strategies.
key_themes: SNCC organizing in Alabama black-belt counties
Lowndes County voter registration and Freedom House
Decision to leave college for full-time movement work
main_summary.historical_significance:
Lawson's account is a primary-source record of the Tuskegee student wing of SNCC's Alabama black-belt strategy in 1965-1967, a relatively under-documented arena compared to the Mississippi work. She names specific co-workers in the Lowndes County Freedom House (Stokely Carmichael, Ralph Featherstone, Courtland Cox, Charlie Cobb, Ivanhoe Donaldson), describes the strategic shift from county-level desegregation work to building black voter pluralities in counties like Lowndes and Wilcox, and recounts the Atlanta arrest by black officers of four SNCC women (herself, Karen Edmonds Spellman, Fay Bellamy Powell, Ruby Doris Smith Robinson), illuminating intra-community policing dynamics during the Black Power era.

Her testimony documents the Birmingham 1963 student walkout from Fairfield Industrial High School under principal Imanu J. Oliver, her direct contact with Charles Gomillion in Macon County voter-rights work prior to his Supreme Court case's full implementation, and the dual-track decision faced by a young Black woman science student who chose movement work over a Cornell medical-school pathway offered by mentors at Sloan-Kettering.

chapters.title: Fairfield Origins and Father's Workshop
chapters.summary: Lawson describes being born in 1946 in Fairfield, Alabama, a Birmingham suburb. Her father, born 1899 in Bullock County near Union Springs, left a 15-child farm family at age 15 for Birmingham, worked the coal mines, then built Lawson's Repair, a backyard mechanical-and-appliance shop. She recounts his self-taught tinkering, his cross-country travel including a much-discussed Birmingham-to-Canada drive on kerosene-converted fuel, and his insistence that she and her brothers learn motor-rewiring and basic repair work.
related_events: Tennessee Coal and Iron company-town strikes

chapters.title: Segregated Fairfield and Community Library
chapters.summary: Lawson describes the totally segregated geography of Fairfield and Birmingham, bus barriers, white-only signage, restaurants accessible only through back windows, libraries with seats removed to prevent integration. She recounts her family's travels to New York and Mexico as breaks from this confined world, and the community library that Mrs. Parham and other women organized in a room above Dr. Parham's pharmacy as a substitute for the closed public library.

chapters.title: First Awareness of Race
chapters.summary: Lawson recalls her earliest race consciousness around age five when she and Teresa Ragusa, a child from the Italian-American grocer family across the street, were told by relatives on both sides that they could no longer play together once they reached school age. She describes hearing adults discuss lynchings, including a Birmingham case of a Black man kidnapped and castrated, where his attackers' kerosene actually clotted the wound and saved his life, and her childhood knowledge of the Ku Klux Klan.

chapters.title: Miles College and Birmingham Black Community
chapters.summary: Lawson describes Miles College as both a struggling historically-black institution and a community playground six blocks from her home. She recounts attending a community screening at Miles of the film 'Imitation of Life', banned from the segregated black Carver Theater, and the multi-generational discussion of passing that followed. She also describes the wealthier Black community on Center Street, including attorney Arthur Shores (whose home was repeatedly bombed) and the Angela Davis family.

chapters.title: Fairfield Industrial High School and Principal Oliver
chapters.summary: Lawson describes the school's hand-me-down books and uniforms inherited from the white Fairfield High School, the Booker T. Washington industrial-education legacy embedded in its name, and the formative role of principal Imanu J. Oliver (also called E.J. Oliver). She describes Oliver running the school 'almost as if it was a private school' with strict standards and homilies including 'dare to struggle, dare to win.' Alumni reunions continue every even year on July 4th weekend.

chapters.title: Family Travels, Mississippi Stop, Arthur G. Gaston
chapters.summary: Lawson recounts a nighttime Mississippi police stop on a westward family trip where her father deflected the officer by calling his children 'little cotton pickers', a subservience that wounded the children but defused the threat. She describes her father's political life in the Republican Party (opposed to the Dixiecrat-controlled Alabama Democrats), his reading of the Pittsburgh Courier and Chicago Defender, and his business partnership with Arthur G. Gaston, who later became one of the first Black millionaires and whose family welcomed Lawson's family to their pool.

chapters.title: Birmingham Demonstrations 1963
chapters.summary: Lawson describes her growing awareness of the civil rights movement through the Montgomery bus boycott, the Freedom Rides arrival in Birmingham, and the 1963 SCLC demonstrations where Martin Luther King Jr. called for student participation. Principal Oliver locked the school doors to prevent her class from leaving; they defied him, walked to downtown Birmingham, and some classmates were arrested or hit by firehoses in Kelly Ingram Park. Lawson herself was not at that location during those events but participated in other demonstrations. Her mother had died by this time; her father initially sided with the principal but later joined her at mass meetings.

chapters.title: Hayden Planetarium and Sloan-Kettering Internships
chapters.summary: Lawson describes summer 1963 and 1964 New York internships in cancer research at the Hayden Planetarium and Sloan-Kettering Memorial Center, arranged through her older half-sisters who worked there as lab technicians. She did blood-analysis lab work using a slide rule, then watched the protocol of discarding samples when a patient died. A Cornell dean among her mentors offered to help her enter Cornell's medical school. Both summers, she felt the conflict of being absent from major civil rights moments, the August 1963 March on Washington and the 1964 Freedom Summer, while in New York.

chapters.title: Tuskegee Entry and Gomillion Voting Rights Work
chapters.summary: Lawson describes entering Tuskegee Institute on a full Gorgas Scholarship (earned via a statewide science-fair sound-wave demonstration) and quickly meeting Almena Lomax (mother of Michael Lomax) and Dr. Charles Gomillion, whose work on Macon County gerrymandering culminated in the Gomillion v. Lightfoot Supreme Court case. Working at the Tuskegee hospital lab, she quit when pressed to practice drawing blood on a woman in labor. She joined an honors program that let her take graduate-level Veterinary Medicine courses including animal parasitology, and met veterinarian Dr. Doris Mitchell, an Odetta friend with an imported sports car.
related_events: Gomillion v. Lightfoot Supreme Court case

chapters.title: Mississippi 1964 Elections and SNCC Tension
chapters.summary: Lawson describes volunteering for SNCC's November 1964 Mississippi voter registration push in Jackson, staying with Euvester Simpson, doing door-to-door canvassing. She recounts the campus tension at Tuskegee when Willie 'Mukasa' Ricks arrived as a SNCC representative and the local student group resisted external direction, while she increasingly identified with SNCC's broader strategic vision over the campus group's downtown-Tuskegee focus. She mentions her classmate Samuel Younge Jr.'s killing as occurring while she was still in school.
related_events: Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party Atlantic City challenge

chapters.title: Selma-to-Montgomery and the Capitol Arrest
chapters.summary: Lawson describes a Tuskegee student delegation marching with picket signs in front of the Alabama State Capitol in Montgomery during the Selma-to-Montgomery period. State troopers slapped billy clubs against their thighs while an unrobed group identified as Klan approached in cars. Troopers arrested all 40-50 demonstrators just before the Klan group reached them. Lawson and her cohort went limp per training and were taken to the Montgomery County jail for about five days, where conditions deteriorated and the group launched a hunger strike. William Kunstler arranged their release.

chapters.title: Atlanta Arrest and Lowndes Freedom House
chapters.summary: Lawson describes a Christmas-season arrest in Atlanta around 1967 with Karen Edmonds Spellman, Fay Bellamy Powell, and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, by Black officers on Auburn Avenue, illustrating intra-community policing dynamics of the Black Power era, with Howard Moore Jr. handling the legal response. She describes leaving school in spring 1966 to work full-time with SNCC, first in Wilcox County (Gee's Bend's Pettway-plantation enclave), then living in the Lowndes County Freedom House, a Jackson-family shack where she slept in a sleeping bag near a pot-belly stove. Late-night conversations with Stokely Carmichael, Ralph Featherstone, Courtland Cox, Charlie Cobb, and Ivanhoe Donaldson engaged Frantz Fanon's writings and Featherstone's economic-empowerment vision, which later informed Mississippi catfish farming and the Fine Vines garment cooperative with Ed Brown and Charles Bannerman in Mississippi Action for Community Education. The transcript ends mid-sentence at 'I worked for...', the recorded interview was truncated before Lawson could complete her account of her subsequent SNCC and post-movement work.

loc_video.caption: Jennifer Lawson oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Washington, DC, District of Columbia, 2015 December 11
